text: This is a robotic Roux-en-Y repair of an E-IV bile duct injury following a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The authors have no relevant disclosures. With over seven hundred and fifty thousand laparoscopic cholecystectomies performed in the US, bile duct injuries continue to be a real problem that requires real solutions. I want to point out the SAGE's Safe Cholecystectomy Program. One salient point, which is relayed here, is that you must clear the lower third of the gallbladder and expose what's called the doublet view, which most of us would be familiar with as the critical view of safety. showing no more than two structures in both the anterior and posterior view.

The video shows a robotic Roux-en-Y repair of a bile duct injury. The surgeon encounters an inflamed gallbladder with pus draining from the gallbladder wall. The omentum and duodenum are adhered due to edema and inflammation. A stapler is fired across the infundibulum of the gallbladder, followed by bile leakage from the cystic plate, which is clipped. An intraoperative cholangiogram is visible on the left side of the screen.

The post-operative HIDA study reveals a significant bile leak from the gallbladder fossa. MRCP shows complete loss of the duct at the bifurcation. During ERCP, free contrast extravasation into the intraperitoneal cavity is observed, with the wire unable to connect with the biliary system. A cholangiogram shows contrast from the liver, with the wire failing to meet the bile duct stent placed during ERCP. Upon entry into the abdomen, a large biloma is encountered, and after cleaning blood and bile, bile duct injuries are noted. The right bile duct is visible, and the left system is cannulated using PTHC placed by IR, aiding in identification.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler and spiral knotless absorbable suture to fashion the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis. The anterior aneurotomy is closed with a running suture, followed by closure of the mesentery defect using a similar technique. The posterior row is completed with a running spiral knotless suture. The left biliary system is tied with an absorbable suture, and the repair is cannulated with the PTHC catheter. The common bile duct, containing an ERCP stent, is closed to prevent leakage, and the pigtail catheter is advanced into the hepatic vaginostomy.

The surgeon completes the anterior row of the hepaticojejunostomy using a Five-O absorbable suture in a running fashion. The right biliary system is sutured closed, and a defect is cannulated with an eight French pediatric catheter, likely the anterior right bile duct. The anterior layer of the hepaticojejunostomy is completed with a running three-O spiraling knotless suture. All mesenteric defects are closed, and the falciform ligament is divided and repositioned to cover the new anastomoses. A drain is placed in the surgical area.

Anterior cholangogram the next day demonstrated that the right posterior system had some free leak and so an internal external catheter was placed. At that point there was good opacification from all three repairs. The patient did quite well and was discharged on post-op day nine. She was seen back in clinic and her intraperitoneal drains were removed. One month out from surgery, an IR cholangiogram was repeated, which showed some slight stenosis of the right posterior bile duct, and the catheter was replaced. Two months out from surgery, she had another IR cholangiogram, which showed that all three bile ducts were completely patent when their catheters were backed up. As such, the catheters were removed. It's worth noting that there are some larger retrospective studies on robotic-assisted HJs now.

In summary, the robotic hepatic ojejunostomy in this patient was not only a safe approach, but may have actually benefited her in discovering and repairing efficiently an injury that was not noted in the preoperative workup. We believe the high definition stereoscopic camera of the robotic platform, as well as the ergonomics and stability, assisted in the repair of this very difficult injury, which would have been highly complex in an open or laparoscopic approach.
